votes.reason: The Python code correctly uses ''.join(strings) to concatenate the list of strings, which matches the behavior of fold_right append in Coq.
The Python code correctly uses ''.join(strings) to concatenate the list of strings, which is exactly equivalent to the Coq fold_right append behavior.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if the result equals the concatenation of the list of strings using ''.join().

The Coq fold_right append "" strings concatenates all strings in the list from right to left, but since string concatenation is associative, this produces the same result as Python's "".join(strings) which concatenates left to right. Both check if result equals the concatenation of all strings in the list.

The Coq fold_right append "" strings concatenates all strings in the list from right to left, which produces the same result as concatenating left to right for string append (since string concatenation is associative). Python's "".join(strings) also concatenates all strings in order, producing the same result for any concrete list of strings.

The Coq fold_right append "" strings concatenates all strings in the list from right to left, which produces the same result as concatenating left to right for string append (since string concatenation is associative). Python's "".join(strings) also concatenates all strings in order, producing the same result for any concrete list of strings.
Both compute the concatenation of the list of strings in order with empty string as identity; this matches Python "".join(strings).
Both require result to equal the concatenation of the list elements (Coq: fold_right append ""; Python: "".join(strings)), preserving order and yielding the same string for all well-typed inputs.
Both define result as the concatenation of the list of strings (empty list yields empty string); Python’s "".join(strings) matches Coq’s fold_right append "" strings.
